sentences.base_language: Early one Saturday morning, Ana decided to go to the neighborhood market.
sentences.target_language: Un sábado por la mañana temprano, Ana decidió ir al mercado del barrio.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Por la mañana** vs **de la mañana**: use **por** when referring to a general part of the day without a clock time: **por la mañana** (in the morning), **por la tarde** (in the afternoon), **por la noche** (at night). Use **de la mañana** when a specific time precedes: **a las ocho de la mañana** (at eight in the morning). **Un sábado por la mañana** (one Saturday morning) — **un** + day-of-week gives 'one particular ~': **un lunes** (one Monday), **una tarde** (one afternoon).
**Temprano** = early — an adverb that stands alone or follows a time expression: **llegó temprano** (arrived early), **por la mañana temprano** (early in the morning). Its opposite **tarde** (late) works the same way: **llegó tarde** (arrived late).

sentences.base_language: She had always loved markets — the colors, the smells, the hustle.
sentences.target_language: Siempre le habían encantado los mercados — los colores, los olores, el bullicio.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le habían encantado** — pluperfect of a gustar-type verb: **encantar** works like **gustar** — the thing loved is the grammatical subject (**los mercados** — plural → **habían**), and the person is the indirect object (**le**). **Siempre** + pluperfect = had always: **siempre le habían encantado** (had always loved them — an enduring past preference). Compare: **le encantaron** (she loved them — preterite, a specific occasion) vs **le encantaban** (she used to love them — habitual imperfect).
**Bullicio** (m.) = bustle, din, hubbub — from **bullir** (to bubble/boil). A vivid word for the lively noise of a crowd: un mercado lleno de **bullicio** (a market full of bustle). Compare: **ruido** (noise — neutral), **alboroto** (commotion — often negative), **animación** (liveliness — more positive).

sentences.base_language: She got dressed quickly and had a coffee before leaving the house.
sentences.target_language: Se vistió rápido y tomó un café antes de salir de casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Antes de + infinitivo** = before doing (same subject): **antes de** salir (before leaving). When the subjects are the same, **antes de** + infinitive is obligatory. When subjects differ, use **antes de que** + subjunctive: **antes de que** él llegara (before he arrived). The same applies to **después de** (after): **después de** comer (after eating — same subject) vs **después de que** ellos comieron (after they ate — different subjects).
**Salir de casa** — no article with **casa** in fixed expressions: **salir de casa** (leave home), **en casa** (at home), **llegar a casa** (get home). The article appears when referring to a specific building: **la casa de mis padres** (my parents' house). A handful of place nouns behave this way in Spanish: **en clase**, **en cama**, **en misa**.

sentences.base_language: The market was only a ten-minute walk from her apartment.
sentences.target_language: El mercado estaba a solo diez minutos a pie de su apartamento.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A + time + a pie** = a ~ walk away: **a diez minutos a pie** (ten minutes on foot). **A pie** (on foot) is the only mode of locomotion that uses **a** rather than **en**: ir **a pie** (walk), ir **en coche** (by car), ir **en bici** (by bike), ir **en metro** (by subway). This exception must be memorized — *en pie* means 'standing up,' not 'on foot.'
**Solo** as adverb (only): the RAE 2010 reform made the accent on **sólo** (adverb) optional — both **solo** and **sólo** are now acceptable. As an adjective (alone), **solo/sola** has never had an accent: **una persona sola** (a person alone). Context almost always clarifies the meaning.

sentences.base_language: When she arrived, the stalls were already overflowing with fresh vegetables and fruit.
sentences.target_language: Cuando llegó, los puestos ya estaban llenos de verduras frescas y fruta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Puesto** (m.) = market stall — from **poner** (to put out): a place where goods are put on display. Regional alternatives: **tenderete** (small outdoor stall), **parada** (Catalonia/Valencia). Compare: **tienda** (shop — permanent premises) and **puesto** (stall — temporary). **Cuando + pretérito** = when (completed entry into the scene: she arrived — discrete event), followed by **ya estaban** (imperfect — the ongoing background state that greeted her).
**Ya + imperfecto** = already (at that point): **ya estaban** llenos (were already full). **Ya** in past contexts = already, by then. Compare: **ya no** (no longer): **ya no** había tomates (there were no longer any tomatoes). **Todavía** / **aún** = still: los puestos **todavía** estaban llenos (the stalls were still full).

sentences.base_language: A farmer was selling tomatoes, peppers, and onions at a very good price.
sentences.target_language: Un agricultor vendía tomates, pimientos y cebollas a muy buen precio.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Vendía** — imperfect for background scene-setting: the farmer was selling (an ongoing activity in the background). This is the classic narrative use of the imperfect — it describes what was already happening when the story arrives. Compare: **vendió** (preterite — sold, a completed transaction; this would advance the plot). The imperfect paints the scene; the preterite drives the action.
**A + precio** = at a price/rate: **a** buen precio (at a good price), **a** dos euros el kilo (at two euros per kilo). This **a** of price/rate is used for speeds and ratios too: **a** cien kilómetros por hora (at 100 km/h), **a** mitad de precio (at half price). Do not confuse with the directional **a** (to): **ir a** casa (go home).

sentences.base_language: She asked him: "How much are the tomatoes?"
sentences.target_language: Le preguntó: «¿A cuánto están los tomates?»
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿A cuánto están?** = how much are they (for sale)? — the standard market-price question. **Estar** is used for prices because they fluctuate: ¿a cuánto **está** la docena? (how much is the dozen?). Compare: **costar** (to cost): ¿cuánto **cuestan**? (how much do they cost?) — both are common; **¿a cuánto están?** is very colloquial and specific to market/shopping contexts.
**Le preguntó** — indirect object **le** (to him): **preguntar** always takes an indirect object for the person asked — never a direct object: **le** pregunté (I asked him/her — never *lo pregunté*). Compare with **pedir** (to ask for/request): **le** pedí un favor (I asked him a favor). Both verbs use indirect objects for the person, a difference from English.

sentences.base_language: He replied: "Two euros a kilo, and they're very ripe today."
sentences.target_language: Él respondió: «Dos euros el kilo, y hoy están muy maduros.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estar maduro** — **estar** for a current, transient state: **están** muy maduros (they are very ripe today — a condition that will change). Compare: **ser** for inherent, stable qualities: esta variedad **es** muy dulce (this variety is very sweet — a fixed characteristic). Ripeness, freshness, and temperature are temporary states → always **estar**: **está** fresco, **está** pasado, **está** maduro.
**Dos euros el kilo** — price per unit: note the definite article **el** (not the indefinite 'a'): **dos euros el kilo** (two euros a/the kilo). Spanish uses the definite article in unit-price expressions where English uses the indefinite: **tres euros la docena** (three euros a dozen), **un euro el litro** (one euro a liter).

sentences.base_language: She bought a kilo of tomatoes and half a kilo of peppers.
sentences.target_language: Compró un kilo de tomates y medio kilo de pimientos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Medio/a** = half (adjective): **medio kilo** (half a kilo), **media hora** (half an hour), **medio litro** (half a liter). **Medio** agrees with the noun it precedes: **medio** kilo (m.), **media** docena (f.), **media** manzana (f.). Compare: **la mitad de** (the half of — noun phrase): **la mitad del** precio (half the price), **la mitad de los estudiantes** (half the students). **Medio** is used directly before the unit; **la mitad de** is used for proportions of a whole.
**Kilo de + noun** — partitive quantity: **un kilo de tomates** (a kilo of tomatoes). The pattern **[quantity] + de + [noun]** is universal: **un litro de leche**, **una docena de huevos**, **una bolsa de arroz**. The **de** simply links the measurement to the substance being measured.

sentences.base_language: At the cheese stall, she tasted a piece of aged cheese and felt very pleased.
sentences.target_language: En el puesto de quesos, probó un trozo de queso añejo y se puso muy contenta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Probar** = to taste, to try (food or things): **probó** un trozo (tasted a piece). Do not confuse with **intentar** (to try to do something — effort). **Probar** takes a direct object: **probar** la sopa (taste the soup), **probar** un vestido (try on a dress). In shops: **¿puedo probarlo?** (can I try it / try it on?). **Añejo/a** = aged, mature — used for cheese, wine, ham: queso **añejo** (aged cheese), vino **añejo** (aged wine). From **año** (year): something that has accumulated years.
**Ponerse + adjective** = to become/get (emotional or physical change): se puso **contenta** (got happy/pleased). **Ponerse** expresses a change into a new state: se puso **roja** (turned red), se puso **nervioso** (got nervous), se puso **triste** (became sad). Compare: **quedarse** (to remain in a state after a change): se quedó **dormida** (fell asleep and stayed that way), se quedó **callada** (went quiet and stayed quiet).

sentences.base_language: The cheesemonger told her that the cheese came from a farm two hours away.
sentences.target_language: El quesero le explicó que el queso venía de una finca a dos horas de allí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Quesero/a** = cheesemonger/cheese dealer — the suffix **-ero/a** is one of the most productive in Spanish for trades and professions: **quesero** (cheese), **panadero** (baker), **carnicero** (butcher), **pescadero** (fishmonger), **verdulero** (vegetable seller), **zapatero** (cobbler). Recognizing this suffix lets you decode and create profession words freely.
**Venía** — imperfect for habitual origin: el queso **venía** de una finca (the cheese came from a farm — habitually, as a standing fact). Compare: **vino** de otra finca esta semana (came from a different farm this week — a single delivery, preterite). The imperfect **venía** describes an ongoing or habitual source; the preterite **vino** would mark a specific occasion.

sentences.base_language: Her arms were getting heavy from carrying all the bags.
sentences.target_language: Los brazos le empezaban a pesar de tanto cargar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Empezar a + infinitivo** = to begin to, to start doing: **empezaban a** pesar (were starting to feel heavy). **Empezar a** is the most natural 'begin to' construction: **empezó a llover** (it started to rain), **empecé a** comprender (I began to understand). Note: **empezar a** (begin to) vs **dejar de** (stop doing) vs **seguir + gerundio** (keep doing): seguía **cargando** (kept on carrying).
**Pesar** intransitively with an indirect object = to feel heavy (to someone): le **pesan** los brazos (her arms feel heavy — lit. her arms weigh to her). This gustar-type construction: the arms are the subject, she is the indirect object. **De tanto + infinitivo** = from so much doing: **de tanto cargar** (from so much carrying), **de tanto caminar** (from all the walking), **de tanto hablar** (from talking so much). A very natural causal expression in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: Suddenly she ran into her neighbor, who was also doing her grocery shopping.
sentences.target_language: De repente se encontró con su vecina, que también estaba haciendo la compra.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Encontrarse con** = to run into, to bump into (someone): se **encontró con** su vecina (ran into her neighbor). The preposition **con** is essential — drop it and the meaning changes completely: **encontrar** algo (to find something). **Encontrarse con alguien** always implies an encounter, often unexpected.
**Hacer la compra** = to do the grocery shopping — a very common fixed expression. **La compra** (the groceries/the shopping) is specifically about food purchasing. Compare: **ir de compras** (go shopping — general, implies browsing and buying various things). ¿Has **hecho** la compra? (Have you done the shopping?) is an everyday household question. **De repente** = suddenly — a fixed adverbial phrase: **de repente** empezó a llover (suddenly it started to rain).

sentences.base_language: They stopped to chat for a while about the market and the neighborhood.
sentences.target_language: Se pararon a charlar un rato sobre el mercado y el barrio.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pararse a + infinitivo** = to stop (in order) to do: se pararon **a** charlar (stopped to have a chat). This **a** expresses purpose — stopping in order to do something. Compare: **parar de + infinitivo** (to stop doing — cease an action): paró **de** hablar (stopped talking). The contrast is crucial: **paró de** trabajar (stopped working — ceased the activity) vs **paró a** descansar (stopped to rest — paused for a purpose).
**Un rato** = a while (an indefinite short period): charlaron **un rato** (chatted for a while). Diminutive **un ratito** adds affection or implies shorter duration: **¡Espera un ratito!** (Wait just a moment!). Compare: **un momento** (a moment — very brief), **un rato** (a while — a bit longer), **un buen rato** (quite a while — longer than expected). **Charlar** = to chat/have a casual conversation — a synonym of **conversar** but more informal.

sentences.base_language: Ana bought some bread at the bakery stand inside the market.
sentences.target_language: Ana compró pan en el puesto de panadería que había dentro del mercado.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Que había** — relative clause with impersonal **haber** in the imperfect: el puesto **que había** dentro del mercado (the stall that was inside the market). Impersonal **había** (there was/there were) describes existence at a past point in time. In relative clauses referring to past background existence, **había** is used: el café **que había** en la esquina (the café that used to be on the corner — may no longer be there).
**Dentro de** = inside (of): **dentro del** mercado (inside the market). Compare: **fuera de** (outside of), **encima de** (on top of), **debajo de** (under), **al lado de** (next to), **enfrente de** (opposite/across from). These prepositional phrases of place follow the pattern **preposition + de** + noun. When **de** + **el** combine: **del** (not *de el*) — a mandatory contraction in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: The smell of fresh bread made her mouth water — she was starving.
sentences.target_language: El olor a pan recién hecho le hizo la boca agua — estaba muerta de hambre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Olor a** = smell of: **el olor a** pan (the smell of bread). The preposition **a** after **olor** introduces the scent: **olor a** café (smell of coffee), **olor a** mar (smell of the sea), **olor a** tierra mojada (smell of wet earth). Compare: **oler a** (to smell like/of): **huele a** pan recién hecho (it smells of fresh bread).
**Hacérsele la boca agua** = to make one's mouth water: le **hizo la boca agua** (made her mouth water — lit. made the mouth water to her). The full reflexive form: **se me hace la boca agua** (my mouth is watering). **Muerta de hambre** = starving — the hyperbolic **muerto/a de** + noun pattern: **muerta de** hambre (starving), **muerto de** frío (freezing), **muerto de** sueño (dead tired), **muerto de** risa (dying of laughter). **Recién + participio** = freshly/just: **recién** hecho (freshly made), **recién** llegado (just arrived).

sentences.base_language: She also paused at a stall selling handmade crafts, though she resisted buying anything.
sentences.target_language: También se detuvo en un puesto de artesanía, aunque resistió la tentación de comprar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Detenerse** = to pause, to stop and linger — reflexive of **detener** (to stop/halt something): se **detuvo** en un puesto (stopped/paused at a stall). **Detenerse** implies a deliberate, reflective pause. Compare: **pararse** (also to stop — more physical/locomotion-focused). **Se detuvo a mirar** (stopped to look) vs **se paró a mirar** — both correct; **detenerse** is slightly more introspective or formal.
**Aunque + indicativo** = although (presenting a conceded fact): **aunque resistió** (although she resisted — this is presented as a fact). Compare: **aunque + subjuntivo** = even if (hypothetical concession): aunque **resistiera** la tentación (even if she were to resist). The same word **aunque** covers both 'although' and 'even if' — the mood (indicative vs subjunctive) makes the distinction. **Artesanía** (f.) = handicrafts, handmade goods — from **artesano** (craftsperson): productos de **artesanía** (handmade products).

sentences.base_language: By midday, her back ached a little from all the walking.
sentences.target_language: Para el mediodía, le dolía un poco la espalda de tanto caminar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para + time** = by (a deadline or reference point): **para el mediodía** (by noon). **Para** vs **a**: **a las tres** (at three o'clock — a point in time), **para las tres** (by three — implying completion by that time). This deadline **para** is essential: **para mañana** (by tomorrow), **para el viernes** (by Friday), **para cuando vuelvas** (by the time you return).
**Le dolía la espalda** — **doler** pattern: the body part (**la espalda**) is the grammatical subject, the person (**le**) is the indirect object. Imperfect **dolía** for an ongoing, sustained pain (it had been aching, as background state). Compare: le **dolió** la espalda al cargar las bolsas (her back hurt when she lifted the bags — a specific moment, preterite). **De tanto caminar** = from all the walking — same causal pattern as **de tanto cargar** in sentence 12.

sentences.base_language: She sat down at a little cafe at the edge of the market and ordered a coffee.
sentences.target_language: Se sentó en un cafetín al borde del mercado y pidió un café.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Al borde de** = at the edge of: **al borde del** mercado (at the edge of the market). **Al borde de** is used both literally (physical edge) and figuratively: **al borde de** las lágrimas (on the verge of tears), **al borde del** agotamiento (on the brink of exhaustion). Compare: **en el borde de** (on the rim/edge — physical, for objects like cups or tables), **a la orilla de** (at the shore/bank of — water contexts).
**Cafetín** = small café — the diminutive suffix **-ín** (also **-ito/a**): **café** → **cafetín** or **cafecito**. Diminutives in Spanish add smallness and often affection or informality. **Pedir** (to order/request, preterite: **pidió**) — note the stem change e→i in the preterite third person: **pidió** (he/she ordered), **pidieron** (they ordered). Compare **pedir** (request — indirect object + **something**) vs **preguntar** (ask a question).

sentences.base_language: She arrived home satisfied — the bags were full and she was in high spirits.
sentences.target_language: Llegó a casa satisfecha — las bolsas estaban llenas y ella tenía muy buen humor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llegó a casa satisfecha** — past participle as adjective: **satisfecha** (satisfied — feminine, agreeing with Ana) used as a predicate adjective after the verb of motion. This is a very natural construction: llegó **cansada** (arrived tired), salió **contenta** (left happy), volvió **decepcionada** (returned disappointed). The participle/adjective describes the subject's state at the time of the action.
**Tener buen humor** = to be in a good mood: **tener** buen/mal humor. In Spanish, **humor** = mood (as in French **humeur**) — a near-false cognate for English speakers who associate it only with comedy. **Estar de buen humor** (to be in a good mood) is equally common: **estaba de buen humor** (was in a good mood). **Tener** buen humor emphasises having/possessing a mood; **estar de buen humor** emphasises being in that state.
